content.text: Based on the resources from Dev.to, Sentry, Tembo, and OpenClaw,
event-driven architecture where an AI coding agent automatically detects production errors from Sentry, analyzes root causes, generates fixes, and creates pull requests

attrs.alt: Webhook-triggered agents (Sentry bug-to-PR pattern) concept diagram

content.text: that monitors production errors and fixes them before users notice:
Sentry (errors) → Webhook → AI Agent → GitHub PR / Hotfix

1. Sentry detects error in production
   ↓
2. Webhook fires when error hits threshold (e.g., >5 occurrences in 10 minutes)
   ↓
3. Agent fetches error details + stack trace from Sentry
   ↓
4. Agent pulls relevant source files from GitHub
   ↓
5. Agent analyzes root cause
   ↓
6. Agent generates fix
   ↓
7. Agent runs test suite against fix
   ↓
8. If tests pass: Open PR (or auto-merge for known safe patterns)
: Vercel serverless function triggered by Sentry webhooks

:
- Fetch error details + stack trace from Sentry
- Pull relevant source files from GitHub
- Analyze root cause
- Generate fix
- Run test suite
- Open PR

Sentry captures error in application
    ↓
Sends webhook to agent (Tembo/OpenClaw/Vercel function)

Agent retrieves:
- Full stack trace
- Error context
- Breadcrumbs
- Environment details
- Source maps
    ↓
Agent analyzes codebase to understand root cause
: Enable rich context in Sentry (source maps, breadcrumbs, custom tags) so agent has more to work with.

Coding agent writes fix in isolated sandbox
    ↓
Runs tests
    ↓
Opens PR with change + full context
: Always require tests to pass. Agent can't merge if it can't prove fix is safe.

:
- Sentry issue link
- Plain-English root cause explanation
- What was changed and why
- Regression tests that reproduce exact error

Human reviews PR
    ↓
Uses feedback loop to refine if needed
    ↓
Merges when ready
: Always review before merging. Use feedback loop to refine fix.

# Install required skills
openclaw install sentry-debugger github-auto-pr slack-digest

:
- Navigate to Integrations page
- Connect Sentry account
- Map Sentry projects to repositories where errors occur

Trigger: sentry.issue.created or sentry.issue.regression
MCP Servers: GitHub + Sentry

Instructions:
When a new Sentry error is detected:
1. Retrieve full stack trace and error context
2. Identify root cause in codebase
3. Write fix and add tests
4. Open PR with clear description of what went wrong and how it's fixed

# On Sentry Error

- Only attempt fix if root cause is clear from stack trace.
  If ambiguous, post diagnostic summary to Slack instead of guessing. [web:202]

- Never fix errors in hot paths (auth, payments, data writes) without flagging for human review
  — add "needs-human-review" label to PR. [web:202]

- If same error keeps recurring after fix deployed, escalate in Slack rather than opening another PR.
  Something deeper is wrong. [web:202]

- Write regression tests that reproduce exact error, not just generic happy-path tests. [web:202]

- Ignore errors that are clearly client-side noise
  (e.g., browser extension interference, network timeouts from mobile). [web:202]
Attackers hijack Sentry alerts to trick AI agents

1. Send fake bug alert to project (need only app's public DSN)
   ↓
2. Fake bug tries to trick coding agent into installing compromised NPM package
   ↓
3. Agent reads external signal as instruction to execute
— external data (fake alert) becomes command agent obeys without verification.
Validate fixes before merging to production
Prove fix is safe before merging
Never auto-fix auth/payments without human review
Always use feedback loop to refine fix
Connect Sentry projects to correct repos, especially monorepos

When Sentry reports error that occurs 5+ times:
1. OpenClaw/Tembo automatically analyzes it
2. Generates fix with tests
3. Opens PR on GitHub
4. Posts summary to #bugs Slack channel
: error context, root cause analysis, and tests.
Run automation every morning at 9:30am, picks issues it's confident can fix cleanly
Wire Sentry User Feedback to Cursor Automation via MCP, agent reads issue and drafts fix
Bugs get resolved before you ever hear about them
Noisy Sentry alerts cause alert fatigue and wasted spend — automation triages and fixes daily
Once configured, workflow is fully automatic
Enables human expert to navigate dense corpus while preserving traceability of sources
Post diagnostic summary to Slack instead of guessing
Escalate in Slack if error keeps recurring after fix
Ignore browser extension interference, mobile network timeouts
an automated janitor for your production system

: Sentry is the alarm that detects a mess (error), the webhook is the door that lets the janitor (AI agent) in, the janitor investigates the mess, cleans it up (fix), and documents what they did (PR) for you to review. The janitor doesn't clean everything — only what it's confident about, and flags dangerous stuff for human help.
Pattern: Sentry → Webhook → AI Agent → GitHub PR/Hotfix for automated bug remediation
Trigger fires when error hits threshold (e.g., >5 occurrences in 10 minutes)
Agent workflow: fetch error → pull source files → analyze root cause → generate fix → run tests → open PR
Always require tests to pass — agent can't merge if it can't prove fix is safe
PR includes: Sentry link, plain-English root cause, what changed and why, regression tests
Only attempt fix if root cause is clear; if ambiguous, post diagnostic summary to Slack
Never auto-fix hot paths (auth, payments) without human review — add "needs-human-review" label
Enable rich context in Sentry (source maps, breadcrumbs, custom tags) for better analysis
: Automatically-triggered agents MUST run in network-sandboxed environments (indirect prompt injection risk)
Test in staging first before merging to production
This is event-driven agent architecture: webhook is the event (Sentry alert), agent is the responder that takes autonomous action (fix), and GitHub is the output channel (PR).
When working with webhook-triggered agents (sentry bug-to-pr pattern), following established best practices ensures reliability, maintainability, and optimal performance.
Start simple and iterate — begin with the minimum viable implementation before adding complexity.
Test with representative inputs — ensure your test cases cover edge cases, not just the happy path.
Monitor performance — track latency, token usage, and error rates in production environments.
Version your prompts and configurations — treat prompt text like source code with proper version control.
Document your design decisions — record why you chose specific approaches for future maintainers.
Plan for failure — implement graceful degradation when the AI component is unavailable or returns unexpected results.
Review outputs regularly — periodically audit what the system produces to catch quality drift over time.
These pitfalls frequently trip up developers working with webhook-triggered agents (sentry bug-to-pr pattern). Knowing them in advance saves significant debugging time.
Assuming deterministic output — language models are probabilistic; the same input can produce different outputs across calls. Always validate the structure of responses.
Ignoring token limits — exceeding context window limits causes truncation or errors. Track token counts, especially in multi-turn conversations.
Over-engineering early — building complex infrastructure before validating basic functionality wastes effort. Get a working prototype first.
Hardcoding prompts in code — embed prompts in configuration files or databases so non-developers can iterate on them.
Skipping error handling — API calls fail due to rate limits, timeouts, or content policy violations. Always wrap calls in try/catch with appropriate retry logic.
As your usage of webhook-triggered agents (sentry bug-to-pr pattern) scales, these performance factors become increasingly important.
Latency budget — typical API latency ranges from 1-30 seconds depending on model size and output length. Design your UX around streaming to provide early feedback.
Concurrency limits — each API tier has request-per-minute (RPM) and token-per-minute (TPM) limits. Implement request queuing for burst traffic.
Prompt caching — use the cache_control breakpoint on stable, repeated prompt prefixes to reduce costs by up to 90% and latency by 85%.
Batching — for non-interactive workloads, the Message Batches API processes up to 10,000 requests asynchronously at 50% cost reduction.
Model selection — use Claude Haiku for high-volume, low-complexity tasks and Claude Sonnet or Opus for complex reasoning where quality matters more than speed.
